questions.question:
A specialized test probe spins on an automated tracking table in a uniform circular loop of radius $0.15\text{ m}$. The onboard instruments register a constant centripetal acceleration of $22\text{ m/s}^2$.

Calculate the frequency of rotation.
A curve of radius $250\text{ m}$ is banked at an angle where $\tan\theta = 0.16$. What is the ideal speed for this curve (no friction needed)? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
A curve of radius $144\text{ m}$ is designed for an ideal speed of $12\text{ m/s}$. What is $\tan\theta$ for this curve? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
On a banked curve taken at the ideal speed, the centripetal force is provided by:
options: The horizontal component of the normal force
The vertical component of the normal force
questions.explanation: At the ideal speed, the horizontal component of the normal force ($F_N \sin\theta$) provides exactly the centripetal force needed. No friction is required.

questions.question: Curve A (radius $r$) is banked at angle $\theta$ and has ideal speed $v$. Curve B has radius $4r$ banked at the same angle. What is its ideal speed?
Two curves have the same radius. Curve X is banked at angle $\theta$. Curve Y is banked at a steeper angle. Which has the higher ideal speed?
options: Both have the same ideal speed
questions.explanation: $v = \sqrt{gr\tan\theta}$. Larger $\tan\theta$ means larger ideal speed at the same radius.

questions.question: A banked curve has $\tan\theta = 0.50$ and an ideal speed of $20\text{ m/s}$. What is its radius? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)

A flat curve of radius $50\text{ m}$ requires a coefficient of friction of $0.80$ to safely round it at $20\text{ m/s}$. If the curve is instead banked at the ideal angle for $20\text{ m/s}$, the required friction becomes:
questions.explanation: At the ideal banking angle, the normal force alone provides the centripetal force. No friction is needed — the curve is self-sustaining at that speed.

questions.question: A race track curve of radius $250\text{ m}$ is banked so that cars can take it at $25\text{ m/s}$ without friction. What is $\tan\theta$? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
A car takes a banked curve at a speed lower than the ideal speed, where friction is absent. Which statement is true?
options: The car slides up the bank
The car slides down the bank
The car continues around the curve safely
questions.explanation: Below ideal speed, the horizontal component of the normal force is greater than the required centripetal force. Without friction to oppose it, the car slides inward and down the bank.

questions.question: Curve P ($r = 100\text{ m}$) has an ideal speed of $20\text{ m/s}$ at angle $\theta$. Curve Q ($r = 400\text{ m}$) is banked at the same angle. What is the ideal speed for Q?
The ideal banking angle $\theta$ for a curve of radius $r$ taken at speed $v$ is given by:
questions.explanation: From the force balance: $F_N\sin\theta = mv^2/r$ and $F_N\cos\theta = mg$. Dividing gives $\tan\theta = v^2/(gr)$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered banked curves and the ideal banking angle!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review $\tan\theta = v^2/(gr)$ and what happens above/below ideal speed.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the relationship between banking angle, radius, and ideal speed.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: why curves are banked, the ideal banking formula, and the role of the normal force.
